items.q: What is a positive climate feedback loop?
items.correct: A process where warming triggers changes that cause more warming — e.g. melting Arctic ice exposes dark ocean, which absorbs more heat, melting more ice (ice-albedo feedback).
items.misconception: Positive feedback means a good outcome for the climate.
items.random_wrong: Positive feedback means the climate balances itself out.
items.why: 'Positive' here means amplifying, not beneficial. Negative feedbacks (which stabilise) are positive for us. The terminology trips students up — it refers to direction of change, not desirability.

items.q: How do humans affect the carbon cycle?
items.correct: By burning fossil fuels (releasing stored geological carbon to the atmosphere) and deforestation (reducing the biosphere store and releasing carbon).
items.misconception: Humans only affect the carbon cycle by breathing out CO2.
items.random_wrong: Humans affect the carbon cycle by eating plants.
items.why: Human respiration is balanced (we eat carbon that plants captured). The disruption is shifting carbon from long-term geological stores into the active atmospheric cycle.

items.correct: The combined loss of water from a surface through evaporation (from soil and water bodies) and transpiration (from plant leaves).
items.misconception: It is only water evaporating from puddles and lakes.
items.random_wrong: It is the process of rain falling from clouds.
items.why: Students focus only on evaporation. Transpiration from forests can move enormous quantities of water — a key concept for understanding deforestation's effect on the water cycle.

items.q: Ice-albedo feedback is an example of:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Positive feedback. “Negative feedback” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The answer examiners want is: 1500. “150” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The real figure is 280. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
